prompt: Explain the attention mechanism in transformer models to a software engineer who has never done ML. Keep it to about 200 words.
response:
The transformer model is a neural network architecture used for natural language processing tasks. At its core is the attention mechanism. Attention allows the model to selectively focus on certain parts of the input when making predictions or generating output.

Imagine you're reading a paragraph and then asked a question about it. You don't reread the whole paragraph to answer - you selectively refocus on the most relevant parts. Attention works similarly in transformers.

Traditional models processed inputs sequentially, passing them through layers one by one. But transformers take an input, generate multiple "queries" from it, and compute an attention score for each query against every input element. These scores determine how much "attention" each input receives when the model makes a prediction or generates output.

This lets transformers weigh different parts of the input based on relevance, allowing them to efficiently handle long-range dependencies between words. Attention scores are learned during training. The attention mechanism is key to the transformative success of models like GPT-3.
